course.section: Event IDs Windows critiques pour le SOC
course.content:
Les journaux Windows contiennent des Event IDs standardisés. Les plus importants :

| Event ID | Description | Catégorie |
|----------|------------|----------|
| **4624** | Connexion réussie | Auth |
| **4625** | Connexion échouée | Auth |
| **4648** | Connexion avec credentials explicites | Auth |
| **4672** | Connexion avec privilèges admin | Auth |
| **4698** | Tâche planifiée créée | Persistence |
| **4720** | Compte utilisateur créé | Account |
| **4732** | Ajout au groupe Administrateurs | Account |
| **7045** | Nouveau service installé | Persistence |
| **1102** | Journal d'audit effacé | Defense Evasion |

Exporter : `wevtutil qe Security /f:CSV > events.csv`

course.section: Parser des exports CSV d'Event Logs
course.content:
```python
import csv
import re
from collections import Counter
from io import StringIO

# Format d'export wevtutil CSV
csv_exemple = """Level,Date and Time,Source,Event ID,Task Category,Details
Information,1/15/2024 10:23:45 AM,Microsoft-Windows-Security-Auditing,4624,Logon,Account: DOMAINE\\admin IP: 192.168.1.5
Warning,1/15/2024 10:24:01 AM,Microsoft-Windows-Security-Auditing,4625,Logon,Account: Administrator IP: 185.220.101.3
Information,1/15/2024 10:24:02 AM,Microsoft-Windows-Security-Auditing,4625,Logon,Account: admin IP: 185.220.101.3
Warning,1/15/2024 10:25:00 AM,Microsoft-Windows-Security-Auditing,4720,User Account Management,New user: backdoor
"""

def analyser_events_csv(csv_content):
    reader = csv.DictReader(StringIO(csv_content))
    stats = Counter()
    alertes = []
    
    for row in reader:
        eid = row.get("Event ID", "")
        stats[eid] += 1
        
        # Événements critiques
        if eid in ["4625", "4720", "1102", "4698", "7045"]:
            alertes.append({
                "event_id": eid,
                "timestamp": row.get("Date and Time"),
                "details": row.get("Details", "")
            })
    
    return stats, alertes

stats, alertes = analyser_events_csv(csv_exemple)
print("Statistiques:")
for eid, n in stats.most_common():
    print(f"  EventID {eid}: {n}")
print(f"\nAlertes ({len(alertes)}):")
for a in alertes:
    print(f"  [{a['event_id']}] {a['details']}")
```

course.section: Parser des exports XML d'Event Logs
course.content:
```python
import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET
from io import StringIO

# Exemple d'event Windows exporté en XML
xml_event = """
<Event xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/win/2004/08/events/event">
  <System>
    <EventID>4625</EventID>
    <TimeCreated SystemTime="2024-01-15T10:24:01.123Z"/>
    <Computer>SERVER01</Computer>
  </System>
  <EventData>
    <Data Name="TargetUserName">Administrator</Data>
    <Data Name="IpAddress">185.220.101.3</Data>
    <Data Name="IpPort">54321</Data>
    <Data Name="LogonType">3</Data>
  </EventData>
</Event>
"""

def parser_event_xml(xml_str):
    ns = {"ev": "http://schemas.microsoft.com/win/2004/08/events/event"}
    root = ET.fromstring(xml_str)
    
    event_id = root.findtext(".//ev:EventID", namespaces=ns)
    time_str = root.find(".//ev:TimeCreated", namespaces=ns)
    computer = root.findtext(".//ev:Computer", namespaces=ns)
    
    # Extraire les données
    data = {}
    for elem in root.findall(".//ev:Data", namespaces=ns):
        name = elem.get("Name")
        if name:
            data[name] = elem.text or ""
    
    return {
        "event_id": event_id,
        "timestamp": time_str.get("SystemTime") if time_str is not None else "",
        "computer": computer,
        "data": data
    }

event = parser_event_xml(xml_event)
print(f"EventID: {event['event_id']}")
print(f"IP Source: {event['data'].get('IpAddress')}")
print(f"Compte: {event['data'].get('TargetUserName')}")
print(f"Type de logon: {event['data'].get('LogonType')}")
```

```python
import csv
import re
from collections import Counter
from io import StringIO

LOGON_TYPES = {
    "2": "Interactive (local)",
    "3": "Network",
    "4": "Batch",
    "5": "Service",
    "7": "Unlock",
    "8": "NetworkCleartext",
    "10": "RemoteInteractive (RDP)",
    "11": "CachedInteractive"
}

def analyser_echecs_4625(events):
    ips_attaquantes = Counter()
    comptes_cibles = Counter()
    types_logon = Counter()
    
    for evt in events:
        if evt.get("event_id") == "4625":
            ip = evt["data"].get("IpAddress", "-")
            user = evt["data"].get("TargetUserName", "-")
            logon_type = evt["data"].get("LogonType", "0")
            
            if ip != "-" and not ip.startswith("127."):
                ips_attaquantes[ip] += 1
            comptes_cibles[user] += 1
            types_logon[LOGON_TYPES.get(logon_type, logon_type)] += 1
    
    return ips_attaquantes, comptes_cibles, types_logon

# Données de test
events = [
    {"event_id": "4625", "data": {"IpAddress": "185.220.101.3", "TargetUserName": "Administrator", "LogonType": "3"}},
    {"event_id": "4625", "data": {"IpAddress": "185.220.101.3", "TargetUserName": "admin", "LogonType": "3"}},
    {"event_id": "4624", "data": {"IpAddress": "192.168.1.5", "TargetUserName": "user1", "LogonType": "2"}},
]

ips, comptes, types = analyser_echecs_4625(events)
print("Top attaquants:", ips.most_common(3))
print("Comptes ciblés:", comptes.most_common(3))
print("Types de logon:", dict(types))
```

exercises.title: Compter les Event IDs
exercises.description: Analyser une liste d'événements et compter les occurrences de chaque Event ID
exercises.instructions:
Soit :
```python
events = [
    {"id": "4625"}, {"id": "4624"}, {"id": "4625"},
    {"id": "4625"}, {"id": "4720"}, {"id": "4624"}
]
```
Affichez le nombre de `4625` (échecs de connexion) :
```
Échecs 4625 : 3
```
exercises.correction:
from collections import Counter
events = [
    {"id": "4625"}, {"id": "4624"}, {"id": "4625"},
    {"id": "4625"}, {"id": "4720"}, {"id": "4624"}
]
c = Counter(e["id"] for e in events)
print(f"Échecs 4625 : {c['4625']}")
exercises.explanation: Counter est parfait pour compter les EventIDs. L'EventID 4625 est le signal d'alerte principal pour les attaques par force brute sur Windows.

exercises.title: Détecter la création de compte suspect
exercises.description: Filtrer les événements de création de compte (EventID 4720)
exercises.explanation: L'EventID 4720 signale la création d'un compte utilisateur Windows. En corrélant avec l'IP source et l'heure, on peut détecter la création de backdoors.

exercises.title: Parser un fragment XML d'Event Log
exercises.description: Extraire l'EventID et l'IP d'un fragment XML Windows
exercises.instructions:
Parsez ce XML et affichez l'EventID et l'IP :
```python
xml = '<Event><System><EventID>4625</EventID></System><EventData><Data Name="IpAddress">10.0.0.99</Data></EventData></Event>'
```
Résultat :
```
EventID: 4625 | IP: 10.0.0.99
```
exercises.correction:
import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET
xml = '<Event><System><EventID>4625</EventID></System><EventData><Data Name="IpAddress">10.0.0.99</Data></EventData></Event>'
root = ET.fromstring(xml)
eid = root.findtext(".//EventID")
ip_elem = root.find(".//Data[@Name='IpAddress']")
ip = ip_elem.text if ip_elem is not None else "N/A"
print(f"EventID: {eid} | IP: {ip}")
exercises.explanation: `find('.//Data[@Name=\'IpAddress\']')` utilise XPath pour trouver l'élément avec l'attribut Name="IpAddress". `xml.etree.ElementTree` est dans la stdlib Python.
